coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of N/P-codoped carbon dots with anti-bacterial activity for enhanced aqueous lubrication performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Guangkai
Cui, Yuhong
Yan, Kaige
Xue, Shenghua
Liu, Shujuan
Ye, Qian
Zhou, Feng
Liu, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: To address energy wastage caused by friction, it is urgently needed to develop multi-functionalized nano-additives based on traditional lubricant additives. Herein, nitrogen/phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) are successfully prepared via one step hydrothermal method using benzopyrrole as raw materials and diethylenetriaminepenta as N, P-dopant. The prepared N,P-CDs demonstrate good lubrication effects when used as water-based additives. Compared with pure H2O, the coefficient of friction (COF) significantly decreases from 0.448 to 0.144, accompanied by a notable reduction in wear volume from 3.84 × 105 μm3 to 2.31 × 105 μm3. This is attributed to the formulation of the robust hydration layer on the CDs surface, as well as the tribochemical protective film generated on the friction pair surface. In addition, the introduction of N,P-CDs effectively inhibits bacterial growth during long-term storage of H2O as lubricant. With small additions (100 μg/mL), the anti-bacterial rates against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus could reach 94.9 % and 99.5 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lubricant additive
Carbon dots
Heteroatom doping
Aqueous lubrication

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20623, U21A2046), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118), Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team (2024RS-CXTD-63) and the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, NPU), China (Grant No. 2023-TS-03).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20623, U21A2046), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118), Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team (2024RS-CXTD-63) and the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing (NPU), China (Grant No. 2023-TS-03).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic structure of the N,P-CDs. TEM images of N-CDs (b) and N,P-CDs (e) (insets: the lateral size distribution). The HR-TEM image of N-CDs (c) and N,P-CDs (f). The AFM image of N-CDs (d) and N,P-CDs (g) (insets: the longitudinal height). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The Raman, (b) FT-IR and (c) XPS full spectra of N-CDs and N,P-CDs. The XPS fine spectrum of (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) P 2p of the two kinds of CDs. (g) The UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of N,P-CDs. (h) The emission spectra with different excitation at of N,P-CDs. (i) The zeta potential of two kinds of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The real-time COF curves and (b) the average COF of the N,P-CDs under different concentration: 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 wt%. (c) The COF of the samples: pure H2O, H2O+1.5 wt% N-CDs and H2O+1.5 wt% N,P-CDs. (d) The variable load test (5, 10 and 15 N) and (e) the variable frequency test (1, 5 and 7 Hz) of the H2O+1.5 wt% N,P-CDs. (f) The average COF of H2O + N,P-CDs under different load and frequency. (g) The 3D and SEM images of the worn scar. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS fine spectrum of the worn surface based on N,P-CDs: (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) P 2p, (d) O 1s, and (e) Fe 2p3/2. (f) The Raman analysis of the worn scar surface. (g) The schematic lubrication mechanism of CDs as lubricant additives. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The growth situations of (a–c) E. coli and (d–f) S. aureus of the agar surface of each group (insets: the SEM images of bacteria each). (g) Statistical results of the two kinds of bacteria attached on the agar surface. (h) The surface potential of the N,P-CDs, E. coli, N,P-CDs + E. coli, S. aureus, and N,P-CDs + S. aureus. (i) The size distribution of the two kinds of bacteria before: d0 and after: d1 co-cultured with N,P-CDs. The scale bar in the SEM images is 500 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of N/P-codoped carbon dots with anti-bacterial activity for enhanced aqueous lubrication performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, 710072, PR China

School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address energy wastage caused by friction, it is urgently needed to develop multi-functionalized nano-additives based on traditional lubricant additives. Herein, nitrogen/phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) are successfully prepared via one step hydrothermal method using benzopyrrole as raw materials and diethylenetriaminepenta as N, P-dopant. The prepared N,P-CDs demonstrate good lubrication effects when used as water-based additives. Compared with pure H2O, the coefficient of friction (COF) significantly decreases from 0.448 to 0.144, accompanied by a notable reduction in wear volume from 3.84 × 105 μm3 to 2.31 × 105 μm3. This is attributed to the formulation of the robust hydration layer on the CDs surface, as well as the tribochemical protective film generated on the friction pair surface. In addition, the introduction of N,P-CDs effectively inhibits bacterial growth during long-term storage of H2O as lubricant. With small additions (100 μg/mL), the anti-bacterial rates against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus could reach 94.9 % and 99.5 %, respectively.

As a water-based lubricant additive, N,P-CDs dramatically enhance the friction-reducing properties of pure H2O (reducing the COF to 0.144), as well as its excellent anti-bacterial activity (over 94 %) contributing to the shelf life for the H2O as lubricant.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Friction can be found extensively in nature, especially evident in the mechanical equipment of modern industry [1]. Although friction plays an important role in transmitting motion and energy, it inevitably creates noise, affects accuracy, reduces reliability and efficiency, and in turn results in significant energy wastage [2]. In accordance with the economic level of individual countries, the annual economic loss due to friction energy consumption is approximately 2–7% of their Gross Domestic Product (GDP) [3]. Therefore, there is an imperative to develop advanced lubrication technologies in order to save resources and reduce energy consumption [4]. Liquid lubrication is an important technique employing the fluid to alleviate friction and reduce wear [5]. Typically, liquid lubricant is consist of lubricating medium and lubricant additives [6]. Amidst the energy crisis and escalating rising environmental worries, H2O emerges as a distinctive environmentally friendly lubricant, offering recyclability and renewability [7]. Due to the self-corrosion of pure H2O and the low viscosity, as well as the bacterial growth caused by long-term storage, its application in industry is limited [8,9]. To meet the demands of complex underwater environment and long-term operation, developing multifunctional lubricating additives with long-term stability represents an effective approach to address this challenge [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs), acting as a novel zero-dimensional (0D) carbon material, represent a family of carbon nanoparticles with the diameters of <10 nm and remarkable structural designability [12]. In addition, the richness of oxygen-containing hydrophilic functional groups (-OH and –COOH), coupled with their extremely small size, inherently renders CDs hydrophilic [13]. Since the initial discovery of CDs in 2004, followed by their incorporation into nanocomposites in 2015 for the anti-friction application, a variety of CDs have been successfully prepared and widely investigated as additives for aqueous lubrication [14,15]. Zhao et al. [16] prepared CDs via one-pot pyrolysis of ammonium citrate as a carbon source, the as-obtained CDs showed excellent hydrophilicity with good dispersion stability, showing the lower coefficient of friction (COF), the less wear rate and the better corrosion resistance. Zhou et al. [17] synthesized CDs through hydrothermal treatment of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and subsequent modification with zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate (ZDS), and the resulting ZDS-functionalized CDs (CQD-ZDS) could reduce the COF of H2O from 0.50 to 0.11, concurrently decreasing wear volume by 44 %. Evidently, the typical approach involves the initial preparation of CDs, followed by a secondary treatment through surface modification, ultimately leading to a significant enhancement in their lubrication performance.

Doping or co-doping with heteroatoms is an effective strategy for altering the electron density of CDs and endow them with excellent performance, often resulting in CDs with excellent physical, chemical, and optical properties [18]. According to the doping atoms involved, these CDs can be categorized into metal doping (such as Ni, Zn, Mg, Ag, and Cu, etc.) and non-metal doping (including N, S, P, Si, and halogen elements, etc.) [18,19]. In the field of tribology, doping with heteroatoms enhances the reactivity of CDs, promotes their adsorption onto the friction pairs surface and then forms a robust tribochemical film during friction, resulting in anti-wear and friction reducing properties [20]. Recent studies indicate that the doped CDs can serve as lubricant additives without requiring additional post-functionalization [21]. Li et al. [22] synthesized various types of CDs doped with metal elements (Mg, Cu, Zn, U, Fe) by one-pot pyrolysis and investigated their tribological performances as aqueous lubricant additives. When 1 wt% of Zn-CDs was added to H2O, it can reduce COF and wear volume by 62.5 % and 81.8 %.

Inspired by the previous design approach, N, P co-doped CDs (N,P-CDs) were prepared through hydrothermal treatment of the heterocyclic compound benzopyrrole (BTZ) and diethylenetriaminepenta (DAP) as N, P-dopant. Good dispersion stability for N,P-CDs in H2O is observed for the prepared N,P-CDs as well as an excellent anti-bacterial activity. Their aqueous lubrication performance as nano-additive is evaluated on the linear reciprocating tribometer (UMT). The influence on their tribological performance of addition concentration, applied load and operating frequency is systematically investigated. Furthermore, the tribological mechanism of N,P-CDs as water-based nano-additives is revealed through surface analysis of worn scar with XPS and Raman techniques.

BTZ (99.5 %) and DAP (50 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Aladdin. Dialysis membrane (width: 44 mm, diameter: 28 mm, molecular weight cut off-MWCO: 3500) was obtained from Hunan Yibo Biotechnology Co. All available H2O used in the experiments was deionized water.

The N,P-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment according to the previous report [23,24]. In brief, 0.54 g of BTZ and 3.98 g of DAP were first dissolved into 30 mL deionized water. The well-mixed solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and subjected to heat treatment in a furnace set at 200 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the obtained mixture underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm and filtration through an aqueous membrane (diameter: 60 mm; pore size: 0.22 μm) to eliminate larger agglomerates. The collected supernatant fluid was then transferred to dialysis membranes (MWCO 3500) and dialyzed for 48 h to remove other small molecule impurities. Finally, the product inside the dialysis membrane underwent freeze-dried, resulting in the acquisition of N,P-CDs (Scheme 1 ). N-CDs were obtained by a similar procedure utilizing BTZ as raw materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The tribological experiments and the anti-bacterial tests
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological performance of CDs was measured at room temperature by a universal friction and wear tester (UMT, USA). The antibacterial efficacy was evaluated using plate counting method, DLS and SEM. All equipment information and the test procedures are provided in the SI part.

Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation of N,P-CDs. Precursor molecule BTZ and the dopant DAP underwent dehydration and polymerization under high temperature and high pressure condition, and finally carbonizing into N,P-CDs. The low-magnification TEM image reveals good monodispersity and absence of visible agglomeration of the prepared CDs (Fig. S2). Fig. 1 presents the structural details and micro-morphological characterization of the prepared CDs. The N-CDs exhibit a predominant lateral dimension of approximately 1.85 nm, whereas the incorporation of P elements leads to an enlargement, with the lateral size expanding to around 2.25 nm (as shown in Fig. 1b and e). Furthermore, HR-TEM image of the N-CDs reveals no obvious crystallization, whereas the lattice fringing is evident in the N,P-CDs (Fig. 1c and f) [25]. The longitudinal height of the CDs was measured by AFM, showing consistency with the lateral dimensions obtained by TEM, and falling with the general range of CDs sizes. The above results indicate that N-CDs and N,P-CDs with near-spherical structures were successfully prepared (Fig. 1d and g) [26].

The microstructure and surface chemical state of the prepared CDs were further explored by Raman, FT-IR, and XPS. The D-region signal (1350 cm−1) and G-region signal (1580 cm−1) of the obtained N,P-CDs are more conspicuous from the results of Raman spectra (Fig. S3 and Fig. 2a). It can be seen that the introduction of the P element enhances the crystallinity of the CDs, in agreement with the HR-TEM results [27,28]. In Fig. 2 b, a significant peak near 3450 cm−1 in CDs corresponds to the stretching vibration of –OH, while the characteristic peak at 3270 cm−1 is originated from the stretching vibration of the amino/amide N–H bond [29]. The vibrational absorption bands at 2916 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are assigned to C–H, and the prominent peak around 1630 cm−1 belongs to the CC stretching vibration within the core skeleton structure in CDs [30]. The peaks appearing around 1450 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 originate from the PO and C–N stretching vibrations of N,P-CDs [24]. A weak absorption peak emerges around 740 cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of CO in –COOH [31]. The above results confirm the successful doping of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) in the CDs. Additionally, the presence of oxygen-containing hydrophilic functional groups (-OH and –COOH) on the surface of N,P-CDs is evident. The full spectra of the CDs and N,P-CDs confirm the presence of the C, O, N and P signals (Fig. 2c), and the corresponding atomic content of the CDs is displayed in Table S2. Notably, successful co-doping of N and P is evidenced by the detection of P signals at 133 and 192 eV, which are absent in the N-CDs, and the atomic percent of P in N,P-CDs is up to 4.06 %. As displayed in Fig. 2d, the C 1s of N,P-CDs has three noticeable peak components located at 288.9 eV(O–CO), 286.6 eV(C–O/C–N), and 284.8 eV(C–C/CC) [32]. The π→π* orbital transition, due to the conjugated structure of the CDs, is responsible for the weak component of 292.2 eV (Fig. S4). [33]. In the N 1s spectrum, two notable characteristic signals emerge at 399.8 eV (pyridine N) and 401.9 eV (graphite N) (Fig. 2e). After the co-doped with N and P, the predominant form of N in N,P-CDs shifts towards pyridine N configuration [34]. In the P 2p spectra, two peaks of 132.8 eV (P 2p3/2) and 133.9 eV (P 2p1/2) is corresponding to C–P (Fig. 2f) [35].

As displayed in Fig. 2g, the ethanol solution containing N,P-CDs exhibits a light yellow under daylight and fluorescence with a blue color under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm). This fluorescence is a prominent characteristic of CDs and serves as a clear indicator of successful preparation [33,36]. As displayed in Fig. S5a and Fig. 2g–N,P-CDs have a maximum excitation wavelength of 335 nm with an emission peak near 465 nm, whereas the optimum excitation wavelength of N-CDs is 385 nm and the emission peak at 430 nm. Unlike typical CDs, the emission wavelengths of both N-CDs and N,P-CDs do not show a direct correlation with the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2h and Fig. S5b). In other words, the fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs remains relatively constant even when the excitation wavelength varies within a certain range, with only intensity difference observed. This indicates that both N-CDs and N,P-CDs are homogeneous in respect to their size and surface structure [37,38]. In Fig. 2i, the surface potential of N-CDs is −17.6 mV due to the abundant electro-attracting substituents (-COOH, –OH, etc.) on their surface [39]. The introduction of P element causes a notable decrease in the surface potential of CDs, from −17.6 mV to −32.7 mV. Incorporating atomic dopants such as N, S, P, and F into the carbon framework significantly alters the electronic structure and surface characteristics of the CDs, consequently modulating the zeta potential [18]. In general, higher values of the zeta potential are indicative of greater stability in colloidal solution systems [40]. The zeta potential results demonstrate that the colloidal aqueous system containing N,P-CDs has an enhanced stability.

It is well known that the concentration of nano-additives significantly affects their performance in the lubrication fluid [41]. In order to obtain the optimal concentration of N,P-CDs, we synthesized the lubricants with different concentration of N,P-CDs (Fig. 3 a and b). Our results demonstrate that effective lubrication is attained at concentrations of 2 wt%, 1.5 wt%, and 1 wt%, with the optimal concentration identified as 1.5 wt%. As shown in Fig. 3c, the COF of pure H2O gradually increases during the run-in stage and finally stabilized around 0.45, with an average COF as high as 0.448. Upon introduction of 1.5 wt% N-CDs into H2O, there is a moderately improvement of COF compared to pure H2O, with the average COF decreasing to 0.413. Whereas, N,P-CDs significantly reduce the COF of H2O, with the average COF dropping to 0.144 (with a reduction of 67.9 %). The N-CDs exhibits a water contact angle of 66.8°, whereas the N,P-CDs samples demonstrates enhanced hydrophilicity with a contact angle of 22.5°, indicating a stronger hydration capacity for the N,P-CDs (Fig. S6). The good lubrication is assigned to the formation of hydration layer on the surface of the N,P-CDs, facilitated by the abundance of hydrophilic groups [42]. To evaluate the effect of applied load and operating frequency on the lubrication behavior of N,P-CDs, real-time COF curves were acquired at varying loads and frequencies (Fig. 3d and e). The results reveal a notable trend of decreasing COF with increasing load and frequency load and frequency. Fig. 3f provides a clearer depiction of the good lubrication of N,P-CDs under the severe operating conditions, which can be attributed to the accelerated dispersion of CDs in the contact zone of the friction pairs, promoted by increased load and elevated operational frequencies. It helps CDs to polish the rough surface and reduce the violent collision of the end face asperities, and ultimately leading to a reduction in COF [17,43]. Simultaneously, N,P-CDs also exhibit exceptional lubricity even under the long-term operation or high load capacity (Figs. S7–8). Fig. 3g and Fig. S9 depicts the 3D model and SEM images of the worn surface after lubrication by different samples. The wear surface based pure H2O is very rough with clearly visible deep furrows and large pits, demonstrating the extremely severe abrasive wear, adhesive wear and corrosive wear. The adhesive wear is slightly decreased in N-CDs based worn surface. After addition of 1.5 wt% N,P-CDs, corrosive wear and adhesive wear nearly vanish, with only trace amounts of abrasive wear. The worn surface based pure H2O presents wide and deep grooves, while the depth and width of the worn scars are notably reduced by the introduction of CDs. Particularly, 1.5 wt% N,P-CDs could help reduce the wear width from 471.7 μm to 280.66 μm and the wear depth from 3.23 μm to 1.70 μm (Fig. S10). In addition, the wear volume of pure H2O is much higher at 3.84 × 105μm3. By contrast, 1.5 wt% of N,P-CDs decreases the wear volume to 2.31 × 105 μm3, which is 39.8 % lower compared to that of pure H2O. Also the surface roughness is minimized after the introduction of N,P-CDs (by 0.421 μm–0.241 μm) (Fig. S11).

In order to further investigate the lubrication mechanism of CDs, XPS analysis and Raman scanning were performed on the worn surfaces. As shown in Fig. S12, the elemental scans of the worn surface have revealed the characteristic signals of C, N, and P. The spectrum in the C 1s region can be divided into three peaks representing O–CO (288.4 eV), C–O/C–N (286.4 eV), and C–C/CC (284.8 eV), respectively (Fig. 4 a). The N 1s displays two major peaks namely C–N (399.9 eV) and –NH (402.2 eV) (Fig. 4b). Clear peaks in the P 2p spectrum corresponding to the PO4 3− component are observed at 132.8 eV (P 2p3/2) and 133.6 eV (P 2p1/2) in Fig. 4c. This suggests that the active element P is involved in the tribochemical reaction. In combination with the results of Fig. 4d–e, it becomes evident that the formation of iron oxide (711.0 eV) and iron phosphate (714.6 eV) has been demonstrated [44]. This suggests that the N,P-CDs deposit on the metal surface and participate in the tribochemical reaction to form a friction protective film. Furthermore, the detection of Si signals implies the existence of a certain quantity of Si3N4 deposited on the worn surface, originated from the upper ball of the friction pairs (Fig. S13). A considerable amount of black substance adheres to the worn surface (Figs. S14a–c). Raman scanning analyses of these worn surface reveal the presence of D and G peak signals similar to those of N,P-CDs, demonstrating the significant deposition of CDs on the worn surfaces. Moreover, the notable signal at around 700 cm−1 is assigned to the formation of iron oxides (Fig. 4f) [28].

From the above analyses, the mechanism for N,P-CDs as water-based lubrication additives can be summarized as follows (Fig. 4g). Firstly, owing to their inherently small size and spherical structure, N,P-CDs swiftly penetrate the contact region of the friction pairs. Secondly, the abundant hydrophilic substituents on the CDs surface assist in the formation of hydrogen bonds with water molecules, promoting the establishment of a stable hydration layer. The hydration layer serves the dual function, preventing the agglomeration of N,P-CDs and facilitating the formation of a stable boundary lubrication film [42]. Thirdly, the negatively charged N,P-CDs are prone to adsorption onto the friction pairs surface through electrostatic interactions, resulting in the effective deposition. Finally, benefiting from the synergistic effect of the active elements N and P, the deposited N,P-CDs undergo the tribochemical reaction induced by the combined factors of rubbing heat and high contact stress [45]. A layer of the tribological protective film composed of carbides, iron oxides and iron phosphates, effectively impedes the direct contact between the friction pairs. In summary, the synergistic effects of the tribological protective film and the hydration layer fostered by N,P-CDs contribute to their exceptional tribological properties as lubricant additives for H2O.

Owing to the inherent corrosiveness of pure H2O and prolonged exposure to external factors, there is a considerable proliferation of bacteria that presents potential hazards to both the stability of the lubrication system and personnel safety [46]. Therefore, the anti-bacterial activity of N,P-CDs in H2O was also examined. Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a Gram-negative bacterium, whereas Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) serves as a typical representative of Gram-positive bacteria. Consequently, these two types of bacteria are selected for testing the anti-bacterial efficacy of the CDs [47,48]. As shown in Fig. 5 a–f, a rapid colonization of bacteria and the conspicuous formation of colonies are observed on control samples. Up the introduction of N-CDs, there is a significant reduction in colony count compared to the control, indicating a substantial anti-bacterial effect of N-CDs. After the N, P co-doping, the anti-bacterial activity of N,P-CDs is further enhanced with a marked decrease in the number of colonies. Statistical calculations of the colony count are performed and shown in Figs. S15–16 and Fig. 5g. The results show that the N,P-CDs have prominent anti-bacterial effect, with the anti-bacterial rate against E. coli and S. aureus reaching as high as 94.9 % and 99.5 %, respectively. The SEM images of bacteria in different groups were also obtained. The bacteria exhibit completely morphologically intact, with smooth cell membranes without any ruptures or wrinkles. Conversely, following treatment with N-CDs, the bacterial surface appears clear wrinkles, and even some cells are completely ruptured. Furthermore, upon exposure to N,P-CDs, the bacteria show ruptured cell membranes and no longer maintain their original rod or spherical shape. Upon contact with the bacterial cell membrane, the abundant functional groups (-NH2, –COOH, –OH, etc.) of CDs tend to interact with the phospholipids of the outer cell membrane, leading to the destabilization of the mechanism and ultimately killing the bacteria [49,50]. A significant change in surface potential of bacteria was also be detected (Fig. 5h). This implies that electro-negative N,P-CDs can successfully attach onto the external surface of bacteria. In addition, the average particle size of N,P-CDs is just 2.25 nm, rendering them highly conducive to penetrating into the interior of the bacterial cells via diffusion to achieve good bacterial inhibition effect. The observed evolution in hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of bacteria before and after co-culture with N,P-CDs shows a dramatic decrease, which indicates the efficacy of N,P-CDs in inhibiting bacterial growth (Fig. 5i). The results have established that N,P-CDs have brilliant anti-bacterial activity and remarkably facilitate the maintenance of water quality stability instead.

In the present work, we have provided a straightforward route for the fabrication of N,P-CDs via a one-step hydrothermal treatment using the benzopyrrole as raw materials and diethylenetriaminepenta as dopant. Benefiting from the advantageous characteristics of their extremely small size (about 2.25 nm) and sub-spherical structure, the prepared N,P-CDs have excellent anti-wear and friction reduction properties as lubricant additives for H2O. Incorporating 1.5 wt% of N,P-CDs into H2O enables a significant reduction in the COF, decreasing from 0.448 to 0.144 (67.9 % reduction), with a corresponding reduction in wear volume from 3.84 × 105 μm3 to 2.31 × 105 μm3. The remarkable lubricating performance is mainly accounted for by the abundance of hydrophilic groups on the N,P-CDs surface, endowing them with excellent hydration ability that facilitates the repairing and filling of pits on the friction pairs. Meanwhile, the successful doping of N and P element promotes the active participation of CDs in tribochemical reactions to form a protective film dominated by carbides, iron oxides, and iron phosphates. In addition to lubricating properties, N,P-CDs endow H2O with remarkable anti-bacterial properties, with high antibacterial rates of 94.9 % and 99.5 % against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. This ensures the long-term stability of H2O as lubricants. A novel approach to the facile preparation of heteroatom-doped CDs is offered in this paper, thus expanding the application of CDs in water-based lubrication.

Guangkai Jin: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuhong Cui: Investigation, Data curation. Kaige Yan: Data curation. Shenghua Xue: Data curation. Shujuan Liu: Writing – review & editing. Qian Ye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Feng Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Weimin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A20623, U21A2046), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118), Shaanxi Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Team (2024RS-CXTD-63) and the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, NPU), China (Grant No. 2023-TS-03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2024.119578.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Compatibility between tribological surfaces and lubricant additives-How friction and wear reduction can be controlled by surface/lube synergies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Neville, A. Morina, T. Haque, M. Voong, Compatibility between tribological surfaces and lubricant additives-How friction and wear reduction can be controlled by surface/lube synergies, Tribo. Int. 40(10) (2007) 1680-1695.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lubricating a bright future: lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Cai, R. Guo, F. Zhou, W. Liu, Lubricating a bright future: Lubrication contribution to energy saving and low carbon emission, Sci. China Technol. Sci. 56(12) (2013) 2888-2913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing metainterfaces with specified friction laws

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Aymard, E. Delplanque, D. Dalmas, J. Scheibert, Designing metainterfaces with specified friction laws, Science 383 (2024) 200-204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of recent advances in tribology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Meng, J. Xu, Z. Jin, B. Prakash, Y. Hu, A review of recent advances in tribology, Friction 8(2) (2020) 221-300.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low friction of superslippery and superlubricity: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zheng, Z. Guo, W. Liu, J. Luo, Low friction of superslippery and superlubricity: A review, Friction 11(7) (2022) 1121-1137.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Macroscale superlubricity with ultralow wear and ultrashort running-in period (∼1 s) through phytic acid-based complex green liquid lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Du, T. Yu, L. Zhang, H. Deng, R. Shen, X. Li, Y. Feng, D. Wang, Macroscale Superlubricity with Ultralow Wear and Ultrashort Running-In Period (∼1 s) through Phytic Acid-Based Complex Green Liquid Lubricants, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 15(7) (2023) 10302-10314.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Renewable lubricants with tailored molecular architecture

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, T.R. Josephson, A. Athaley, Q.P. Chen, A. Norton, M. Ierapetritou, J.I. Siepmann, B. Saha1, D.G. Vlachos, Renewable lubricants with tailored molecular architecture, Sci. Adv. 5(2) (2019) eaav5487.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-based lubricants: development, properties, and performances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.H. Rahman, H. Warneke, H. Webbert, J. Rodriguez, E. Austin, K. Tokunaga, D.K. Rajak, P.L. Menezes, Water-Based Lubricants: Development, Properties, and Performances, Lubricants 9(8) (2021) 73.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Robust and durable superlubricity enabled by carboxylated graphene quantum dots in lubricated steel contacts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Nadeem, M. Finsgar, G. Drazic, M. Malok, A. Morina, M. Kalin, Robust and durable superlubricity enabled by carboxylated graphene quantum dots in lubricated steel contacts, Carbon 226 (2024) 119226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis, biomedical and biotechnological applications of carbon and graphene quantum dots. A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Iravani, R.S. Varma, Green synthesis, biomedical and biotechnological applications of carbon and graphene quantum dots. A review, Environ. Chem. Lett. 18(3) (2020) 703-727.
J. Zhao, Y. Huang, Y. He, Y. Shi, Nanolubricant additives: A review, Friction 9(5) (2020) 891-917.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A multifunctional chemical toolbox to engineer carbon dots for biomedical and energy applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. DHordevic, F. Arcudi, M. Cacioppo, M. Prato, A multifunctional chemical toolbox to engineer carbon dots for biomedical and energy applications, Nat. Nanotechnol. 17(2) (2022) 112-130.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties of carbon dots versus small molecules from “bottom-up” synthesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Bian, A. Wallum, A. Mehmood, E. Gomez, Z. Wang, S. Pandit, S. Nie, S. Link, B.G. Levine, M. Gruebele, Properties of Carbon Dots versus Small Molecules from “Bottom-up” Synthesis, ACS Nano 17(22) (2023) 22788-22799.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, R. Ray, Y. Gu, H.J. Ploehn, L. Gearheart, K. Raker, W.A. Scrivens, Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube, J. Am. Chem. Soc 126 (2004) 12736-12737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot/CuSxnanocomposites towards highly efficient lubrication and metal wear repair

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Huang, H. Hu, S. Qiao, L. Bai, M. Han, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Carbon quantum dot/CuSxnanocomposites towards highly efficient lubrication and metal wear repair, Nanoscale 7(26) (2015) 11321-11327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot pyrolysis preparation of carbon dots as eco-friendly nanoadditives of water-based lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hu, Y. Wang, C. Wang, Y. Ye, H. Zhao, J. Li, X. Lu, C. Mao, S. Chen, J. Mao, L. Wang, Q. Xue, One-pot pyrolysis preparation of carbon dots as eco-friendly nanoadditives of water-based lubricants, Carbon 152 (2019) 511-520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate functionalized carbon quantum dots as water-based lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xue, Y. Cui, X. Chen, J. Shi, S. Liu, Q. Ye, F. Zhou, Zwitterionic dopamine sulfonate functionalized carbon quantum dots as water-based lubricant additive for friction and wear reduction, Appl. Surf. Sci. 615 (2023) 156411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Xu, T. Kuang, Y. Liu, L. Cai, X. Peng, T. Sreenivasan Sreeprasad, P. Zhao, Z. Yu, N. Li, Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications, J. Mater. Chem. B 4(45) (2016) 7204-7219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots for sensing applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Sohal, B. Maity, S. Basu, Recent advances in heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots for sensing applications, RSC Adv. 11(41) (2021) 25586-25615.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of doped carbon quantum dot-based nanomaterials for lubricant additive applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.B. Kumar, A.K. Sahu, K.B.S. Rao, Development of Doped Carbon Quantum Dot-Based Nanomaterials for Lubricant Additive Applications, Lubricants 10(7) (2022) 144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of carbon quantum dots in lubricant additives: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Tang, Z. Zhang, Y. Li, Applications of carbon quantum dots in lubricant additives: a review, J. Mater. Sci. 56(21) (2021) 12061-12092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribological performance of various metal-doped carbon dots as water-based lubricant additives and their potential application as additives of poly(ethylene glycol)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Tang, X. Zhu, Y. Li, Tribological performance of various metal-doped carbon dots as water-based lubricant additives and their potential application as additives of poly(ethylene glycol), Friction 10(5) (2021) 688-705.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N, P-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for carbendazim detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, D. Huo, H. Wu, X. Wang, J. Yang, M. Bian, Y. Ma, C. Hou, N, P-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for carbendazim detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer, Sens. Actuators, B 274 (2018) 296-303.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly N,P-doped carbon dots: rational design, photoluminescence and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, Y. Jiao, L. Feng, Y. Zhong, G. Zuo, A. Xie, W. Dong, Highly N,P-doped carbon dots: Rational design, photoluminescence and cellular imaging, Microchimica Acta 184(8) (2017) 2933-2940.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light-excited ultralong-lifetime room temperature phosphorescence based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots for double anticounterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, H. Zhang, S. Shuang, C. Dong, Visible-Light-Excited Ultralong-Lifetime Room Temperature Phosphorescence Based on Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots for Double Anticounterfeiting, Adv. Opt. Mater 8(7) (2020) 1901557.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, X. He, Z. Kang, H. Huang, Y. Liu, J. Liu, S. Lian, C.H.A. Tsang, X. Yang, S.-T. Lee, Water-Soluble Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots and Photocatalyst Design, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 49(26) (2010) 4430-4434.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Raman spectroscopy of carbon materials and their composites: graphene, nanotubes and fibres

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, L. Deng, I.A. Kinloch, R.J. Young, Raman spectroscopy of carbon materials and their composites: Graphene, nanotubes and fibres, Prog. Mater. Sci. 135 (2023) 101089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Raman spectroscopic studies of the corrosion of model iron electrodes in sodium chloride solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Larroumet, D. Greenfield, R. Akid, J. Yarwood, Raman spectroscopic studies of the corrosion of model iron electrodes in sodium chloride solution, J. Raman Spectrosc. 38(12) (2007) 1577-1585.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ pyrolysis synthesis of multi-element (F, N, S) co-doped porous carbon nanospheres as lubricating additives with improved tribological behaviors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, S. Liu, Y. Cui, W. Bai, S. Liu, Q. Ye, F. Zhou, W. Liu, In-situ pyrolysis synthesis of multi-element (F, N, S) co-doped porous carbon nanospheres as lubricating additives with improved tribological behaviors, Carbon 218 (2024) 118724.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong lifetime and efficient room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots through multi-confinement structure design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, S. Liu, L. Sun, S. Wu, G. Hu, X. Pang, A.T. Smith, C. Hu, S. Zeng, W. Wang, Y. Liu, M. Zheng, Ultralong lifetime and efficient room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots through multi-confinement structure design, Nat. Commun. 11(1) (2020) 5591.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhang, W. Li, K. Huang, H. Guo, Z. Li, Y. Fang, R.M. Yadav, V. Shanov, P.M. Ajayan, L. Wang, C. Lian, J. Wu, Regulation of functional groups on graphene quantum dots directs selective CO2 to CH4 conversion, Nat. Commun. 12(1) (2021) 5265.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Jin, S. Xue, R. Zhang, S. Liu, S. Wang, S. Liu, Q. Ye, H. Wang, F. Zhou, W. Liu, Pulsed Laser Manufactured Heteroatom Doped Carbon Dots via Heterocyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons for Improved Tribology Performance, Small 20 (2024) 2311876.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jian, S. Wang, Q. Ye, F. Li, G. Su, W. Liu, C. Qu, F. Liu, C. Li, L. Jia, A.A. Novikov, V.A. Vinokurov, D.H.S. Harvey, D. Shchukin, D. Friedrich, R. van de Krol, H. Wang, Activating a Semiconductor-Liquid Junction via Laser-Derived Dual Interfacial Layers for Boosted Photoelectrochemical Water Splitting, Adv Mater 34(19) (2022) e2201140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-phosphorus codoped carbon nanospheres as lubricant additives for antiwear and friction reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Ye, S. Liu, F. Xu, J. Zhang, S. Liu, W. Liu, Nitrogen-Phosphorus Codoped Carbon Nanospheres as Lubricant Additives for Antiwear and Friction Reduction, ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 3(6) (2020) 5362-5371.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.W. Liu, F. Peng, H.J. Wang, H. Yu, W.X. Zheng, J. Yang, Phosphorus-Doped Graphite Layers with High Electrocatalytic Activity for the O2 Reduction in an Alkaline Medium, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 50(14) (2011) 3257-3261.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, X. Chai, X. Fu, G. Mao, H. Wang, Fabrication of nitrogen-enriched carbon dots with green fluorescence for enzyme-free detection of uric acid, New J. Chem. 46 (2022) 15367-15372

Liu, Dynamic Directional Ultrasonically In Situ-Generated N,S-Codoped Carbon Dots in Poly(ethylene glycol) for Improved Tribological Performance, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 16(30) (2024) 39896-39905.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xue, Y. Cui, G. Jin, H. Wang, S. Liu, Q. Ye, H. Wang, F. Zhou, W. Liu, Carbon Dots with Spatially-Mediated-N/S-Co-Doping Enabling One-Year Stable Lubricant with Oil Leakage Detection Capability, Small 20 (2024) 2312010.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Biesinger, B.P. Payne, A.P. Grosvenor, L.W.M. Lau, A.R. Gerson, R.S.C. Smart, Resolving surface chemical states in XPS analysis of first row transition metals, oxides and hydroxides: Cr, Mn, Fe, Co and Ni, Appl. Surf. Sci. 257(7) (2011) 2717-2730.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ge, J. Li, C. Zhang, Y. Liu, J. Luo, Superlubricity and Antiwear Properties of In Situ-Formed Ionic Liquids at Ceramic Interfaces Induced by Tribochemical Reactions, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 11(6) (2019) 6568-6574.
